expanded_description: Compare and contrast ionic and covalent compounds across physical and chemical properties, understanding how bonding type determines melting point, boiling point, conductivity, solubility, and reactivity patterns.

core_concept.explanation: The type of chemical bonding (ionic vs. covalent) fundamentally determines a compound's physical and chemical properties. Ionic compounds form crystal lattices with strong electrostatic forces, while covalent compounds form discrete molecules with weaker intermolecular forces. These structural differences manifest in dramatically different melting points, conductivity, solubility, and mechanical properties.

image.alt: Side-by-side comparison of ionic lattice and covalent molecular structures
image.caption: Figure 6.10.1: Ionic compounds form extended crystal lattices with strong electrostatic forces; covalent compounds form discrete molecules with weaker intermolecular forces.

sections.introduction: The fundamental structural difference between ionic and covalent compounds explains most of their property differences. Ionic compounds form extended three-dimensional crystal lattices, while covalent compounds exist as discrete molecules.

key_concept.definition: Ionic compounds consist of alternating cations and anions in a repeating crystal lattice structure with no discrete molecules. Covalent compounds consist of discrete molecules with atoms held together by shared electron pairs.
key_concept.context: This structural difference is the root cause of property variations in melting point, conductivity, solubility, and mechanical behavior.

characteristics: Each ion surrounded by oppositely charged ions
classifications.behavior: The entire crystal acts as one giant 'molecule' held together by electrostatic forces in all directions.

examples.explanation: Each $\text{Na}^+$ surrounded by 6 $\text{Cl}^-$; each $\text{Cl}^-$ surrounded by 6 $\text{Na}^+$ in face-centered cubic lattice.

image.caption: NaCl formula unit represents 1:1 ratio, not a discrete molecule.

examples.explanation: Each $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ surrounded by 8 $\text{F}^-$; each $\text{F}^-$ surrounded by 4 $\text{Ca}^{2+}$.

characteristics: Distinct molecules with specific atom counts
Molecular formula shows actual atom counts
classifications.behavior: Molecules maintain their identity in all phases; intermolecular forces determine bulk properties.

examples.explanation: Each water molecule has exactly 2 H and 1 O atom; molecules interact via H-bonding.

image.caption: Discrete H₂O molecules with intermolecular H-bonds.

examples.explanation: Each carbon dioxide molecule has 1 C and 2 O atoms; molecules interact via London dispersion forces.

image.caption: Discrete CO₂ molecules with weak intermolecular forces.

characteristics: Atoms connected by covalent bonds in extended network
classifications.behavior: These covalent compounds behave more like ionic compounds in terms of melting point and hardness due to extended bonding.

examples.explanation: Each carbon covalently bonded to 4 others in tetrahedral network; mp $> 3500^\circ\text{C}$.
Each Si bonded to 4 O; each O bonded to 2 Si in continuous network; mp $1710^\circ\text{C}$.

principles.statement: Ionic compound formulas represent the simplest whole-number ratio of ions (formula unit); covalent compound formulas represent the actual number of atoms in a molecule.
principles.application: $\text{NaCl}$ means 1:1 ratio of ions in lattice; $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ means exactly 2 H and 1 O per molecule.
principles.analogy: Like a recipe ratio (ionic) vs. a complete dish (covalent): '2 cups flour : 1 cup sugar' vs. 'one cake with 2 eggs'.

sections.introduction: Melting and boiling points reflect the strength of forces that must be overcome to change phase. Ionic compounds generally have much higher melting and boiling points than covalent molecular compounds.

key_concept.definition: Melting point is the temperature at which a solid becomes liquid; boiling point is the temperature at which a liquid becomes gas. These temperatures reflect the energy needed to overcome interparticle forces.
key_concept.context: Ionic compounds require breaking strong electrostatic forces; covalent molecular compounds require overcoming weaker intermolecular forces.

components.interpretation: Covalent molecular: Overcome intermolecular forces $(5-50~\text{kJ/mol})$; covalent bonds within molecules remain intact.

image.alt: Chart comparing melting and boiling points of ionic vs covalent compounds
image.caption: Figure 6.10.2: Ionic compounds have dramatically higher melting and boiling points than covalent molecular compounds.

categories.description: Ionic compounds typically melt above $400^\circ\text{C}$, many above $800^\circ\text{C}$, due to strong electrostatic lattice forces.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Melting requires overcoming the lattice energy—the energy holding ions in the crystal structure. Higher ion charges and smaller ion sizes increase lattice energy.

examples.explanation: Melting point: $801^\circ\text{C}$; Boiling point: $1413^\circ\text{C}$. Strong $\text{Na}^+$-$\text{Cl}^-$ electrostatic attraction.

image.caption: High melting point reflects strong ionic bonding.

examples.explanation: Melting point: $2852^\circ\text{C}$; Higher than NaCl due to $\text{Mg}^{2+}$ and $\text{O}^{2-}$ (double charges).

image.caption: Doubly charged ions create stronger lattice.

examples.explanation: Melting point: $825^\circ\text{C}$ (decomposes); Polyatomic ions still create strong ionic lattice.

categories.description: Covalent molecular compounds typically melt below $300^\circ\text{C}$, many below $0^\circ\text{C}$, due to weak intermolecular forces.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Melting only requires overcoming intermolecular forces (LDF, dipole-dipole, H-bonding); covalent bonds within molecules remain intact.

examples.explanation: Melting point: $0^\circ\text{C}$; Boiling point: $100^\circ\text{C}$. High for covalent due to H-bonding.

image.caption: H-bonding raises mp above typical covalent compounds.

examples.explanation: Sublimes at $-78^\circ\text{C}$; Only London dispersion forces between molecules.

image.caption: Weak LDF gives very low sublimation point.

examples.explanation: Melting point: $-182^\circ\text{C}$; Boiling point: $-161^\circ\text{C}$. Only LDF between small nonpolar molecules.

image.caption: Very low mp reflects weak intermolecular forces.

categories.description: Covalent network solids have extremely high melting points, comparable to or exceeding ionic compounds, because covalent bonds must be broken.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Melting requires breaking actual covalent bonds throughout the network, not just intermolecular forces.

examples.explanation: Melting point: $> 3500^\circ\text{C}$ (at pressure); Every atom covalently bonded to neighbors.

image.caption: Breaking covalent bonds requires extreme energy.

examples.explanation: Melting point: $2830^\circ\text{C}$ (decomposes); Used in high-temperature applications.

principles.statement: For ionic compounds, melting point correlates with lattice energy: $U \propto \dfrac{Q_1 Q_2}{r}$, where $Q$ are ion charges and $r$ is ion separation.
principles.application: $\text{MgO}$ (mp $2852^\circ\text{C}$) melts higher than $\text{NaCl}$ (mp $801^\circ\text{C}$) because $\text{Mg}^{2+}$/$\text{O}^{2-}$ have double the charge of $\text{Na}^+$/$\text{Cl}^-$.
principles.example: This explains why ionic compounds with multiply-charged ions have exceptionally high melting points.

principles.statement: For covalent molecular compounds, melting point increases with intermolecular force strength: LDF < dipole-dipole < H-bonding.
principles.analogy: Like different adhesives: weak tape (LDF) vs. sticky notes (dipole) vs. superglue (H-bond)—stronger adhesion requires more force to separate.

sections.introduction: Electrical conductivity depends on the presence of mobile charged particles. Ionic and covalent compounds show dramatically different conductivity behavior depending on their physical state.

key_concept.definition: Electrical conductivity requires mobile charge carriers (electrons or ions). Ionic compounds conduct when molten or dissolved but not as solids; covalent compounds generally don't conduct in any state.
key_concept.context: This property is used to experimentally distinguish ionic from covalent compounds in the laboratory.

characteristics: Ions fixed in crystal lattice positions
classifications.behavior: Despite containing charged particles, solid ionic compounds don't conduct because ions cannot move from their lattice positions.

examples.explanation: Ions locked in place; no current flow even with applied voltage.

characteristics: Ions free to move in liquid
classifications.behavior: When melted, the lattice breaks down and ions become mobile charge carriers, enabling electrical conduction.

examples.explanation: $\text{Na}^+$ moves to cathode, $\text{Cl}^-$ moves to anode; current flows through liquid.
Used in aluminum production (Hall-Héroult process); $\text{Al}^{3+}$ reduced at cathode to form Al metal.

image.caption: Industrial electrolysis of molten ionic compound.

classifications.behavior: Dissolved ionic compounds form electrolyte solutions; strong electrolytes dissociate completely.

examples.explanation: $\text{NaCl}(s) \rightarrow \text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq)$; solution conducts electricity.
Blue solution conducts; used in electroplating where $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ is reduced to Cu metal.

classifications.behavior: Covalent molecular compounds lack mobile charge carriers in any state; electrons are localized in bonds.

examples.explanation: $\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{22}\text{O}_{11}(aq)$ doesn't conduct; molecules don't dissociate into ions.
Very low conductivity ($5.5 \times 10^{-6}~\text{S/m}$); minimal autoionization to $\text{H}^+$ and $\text{OH}^-$.

image.caption: Minimal ionization gives very low conductivity.

classifications.behavior: Covalent network solids show varied conductivity based on electronic structure, not bonding type alone.

examples.explanation: Conducts along planes due to delocalized $\pi$ electrons; used in electrodes and pencils.
Excellent insulator; all 4 valence electrons in $\sigma$ bonds; no mobile charge carriers.

principles.statement: Testing electrical conductivity in aqueous solution is a practical method to distinguish ionic from covalent compounds.
principles.application: If a water solution conducts electricity, the compound is likely ionic (or a polar covalent acid/base that ionizes). If it doesn't conduct, it's likely covalent molecular.
principles.analogy: Like a litmus test for bonding: conductivity = ionic (usually); no conductivity = covalent (usually).

principles.statement: Ionic compounds are strong electrolytes (100% dissociation); polar covalent acids/bases are weak electrolytes (partial ionization); nonpolar covalent are non-electrolytes.
principles.example: This classification is essential for understanding solution chemistry and electrochemistry.

sections.introduction: Solubility depends on the compatibility of solute-solvent intermolecular forces. Ionic and covalent compounds follow different solubility patterns based on their bonding and polarity.

key_concept.definition: Solubility is the maximum amount of solute that can dissolve in a solvent at a given temperature. 'Like dissolves like': polar/ionic solutes dissolve in polar solvents; nonpolar solutes dissolve in nonpolar solvents.
key_concept.context: Water is the most common solvent; understanding solubility in water is essential for chemistry and biology.

categories.description: Many ionic compounds are soluble in water due to favorable ion-dipole interactions between ions and water molecules.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Water molecules surround ions (hydration), with $\text{O}^{\delta-}$ oriented toward cations and $\text{H}^{\delta+}$ toward anions. Hydration energy must overcome lattice energy for dissolution.

examples.explanation: Highly soluble ($360~\text{g/L}$ at $25^\circ\text{C}$); hydration energy exceeds lattice energy.
Insoluble ($0.002~\text{g/L}$); lattice energy exceeds hydration energy.

categories.description: General rules predict which ionic compounds are soluble in water based on ion combinations.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Solubility depends on the balance between lattice energy (favors solid) and hydration energy (favors solution). Certain ion combinations consistently favor one or the other.

examples.explanation: $\text{Na}^+$, $\text{K}^+$, $\text{NH}_4^+$, $\text{NO}_3^-$ salts are always soluble regardless of counterion.

image.caption: These ions always form soluble compounds.

examples.explanation: $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$, $\text{PO}_4^{3-}$, $\text{S}^{2-}$ salts are insoluble except with Group 1 or $\text{NH}_4^+$.

categories.description: Polar covalent compounds with H-bonding capability are often soluble in water through H-bond and dipole-dipole interactions.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Water forms H-bonds with polar functional groups ($-\text{OH}$, $-\text{NH}_2$, $\text{C}=\text{O}$), stabilizing dissolved molecules.

examples.explanation: Miscible with water; $-\text{OH}$ group forms H-bonds with water molecules.
Highly soluble ($909~\text{g/L}$); multiple $-\text{OH}$ groups form extensive H-bonds with water.

image.caption: Multiple H-bond sites enable high solubility.

categories.description: Nonpolar covalent compounds are insoluble in water but soluble in nonpolar solvents (hexane, benzene, $\text{CCl}_4$).
categories.detailed_mechanism: Water-water H-bonds are stronger than water-nonpolar interactions; nonpolar molecules prefer LDF interactions with each other or with nonpolar solvents.

examples.explanation: Immiscible; oil molecules cluster together, excluding water (hydrophobic effect).

image.caption: Nonpolar oil doesn't mix with polar water.

examples.explanation: $\text{I}_2$ dissolves readily in $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{14}$ (nonpolar) but not in water; LDF-LDF interactions compatible.

principles.statement: Substances with similar intermolecular forces mix well; substances with different intermolecular forces tend to separate.
principles.application: Ionic/polar solutes + polar solvents (water) = soluble; Nonpolar solutes + nonpolar solvents (hexane) = soluble; Mixed = insoluble.
principles.analogy: Like social groups: similar people (polarities) interact comfortably; very different people tend to stay separate.

principles.statement: Most solid solutes become more soluble with increasing temperature; gas solubility decreases with increasing temperature.
principles.application: Sugar dissolves better in hot tea than cold; warm soda goes flat faster because $\text{CO}_2$ escapes more readily.
principles.example: This principle is important for crystallization, recrystallization purification, and understanding environmental gas exchange.

sections.introduction: The way compounds respond to mechanical stress (hammering, cutting, pressing) depends on their bonding and structure. Ionic and covalent compounds show characteristic mechanical behaviors.

key_concept.definition: Hardness is resistance to scratching or deformation; brittleness is tendency to fracture rather than deform. These properties reflect how bonds respond to applied stress.
key_concept.context: Mechanical properties determine practical applications: construction materials, cutting tools, jewelry, etc.

characteristics: Hard: ions held tightly in lattice
classifications.behavior: When stress shifts ion layers, like-charged ions align and repel, causing the crystal to cleave along planes.

examples.explanation: Hard enough to scratch some materials, but shatters when struck; cleaves along cubic planes.

image.caption: Ionic crystals cleave along lattice planes.

examples.explanation: Mohs hardness 3; rhombohedral cleavage; used in construction but not structural applications.

image.caption: Characteristic cleavage pattern in ionic minerals.

characteristics: Molecules slide past each other easily
Low melting points correlate with softness
Many are gases or liquids at room temperature
classifications.behavior: Molecular solids can be cut or deformed easily because only weak intermolecular forces must be overcome.

examples.explanation: Long hydrocarbon molecules held by LDF; easily cut, melts at $50-60^\circ\text{C}$.

image.caption: Weak LDF gives soft, easily deformed solid.

examples.explanation: Can be cut with knife; sublimes at $-78^\circ\text{C}$; very weak intermolecular forces.

image.caption: Molecular solid with very weak forces.

characteristics: Atoms connected by strong covalent bonds
classifications.behavior: Breaking requires breaking actual covalent bonds, not just intermolecular forces, giving exceptional hardness.

examples.explanation: Mohs hardness 10 (hardest natural material); used in cutting tools, drill bits, jewelry.
Mohs hardness 7; used in glassmaking, abrasives, electronics (piezoelectric properties).

classifications.behavior: When stress is applied, metal ion layers slide past each other; electron sea readjusts to maintain bonding.

examples.explanation: Can be hammered to $< 0.1~\mu\text{m}$ thickness without breaking; most malleable metal.
Drawn into thin wires for electrical applications; ductile due to metallic bonding.

principles.statement: When ionic crystals are struck, ion layers shift, bringing like-charged ions adjacent; electrostatic repulsion causes fracture.
principles.application: This explains why ionic compounds shatter rather than bend; the mechanism is fundamentally different from metallic deformation.
principles.analogy: Like stacking magnets: if you shift the stack so north faces north, they repel and fly apart.

principles.statement: Mohs hardness scale (1-10) ranks minerals by scratch resistance; covalent network solids occupy the high end.
principles.application: Talc (1) < Gypsum (2) < Calcite (3) < Fluorite (4) < Apatite (5) < Orthoclase (6) < Quartz (7) < Topaz (8) < Corundum (9) < Diamond (10).
principles.example: This scale is used in geology, materials science, and jewelry to characterize material hardness.

sections.introduction: Apply understanding of ionic vs. covalent properties to identify compound types, predict properties, and explain observed behaviors.

worked_examples.title: Identifying Compound Type from Properties
worked_examples.problem: A white crystalline solid has the following properties: mp $801^\circ\text{C}$, conducts electricity when molten but not as solid, soluble in water. Is it ionic or covalent? Explain.

step_by_step.explanation: mp $801^\circ\text{C}$ is high, typical of ionic compounds. Covalent molecular compounds usually melt below $300^\circ\text{C}$.
Conducts when molten but not solid is the hallmark of ionic compounds. Covalent compounds don't conduct in either state.
Soluble in water is consistent with many ionic compounds (though some covalent polar compounds also dissolve).
All three properties point to ionic bonding. The compound is likely $\text{NaCl}$ (matches all properties exactly).

worked_examples.final_answer: The compound is ionic. High melting point ($801^\circ\text{C}$), conductivity only when molten (mobile ions), and water solubility are all characteristic of ionic compounds. These properties match sodium chloride ($\text{NaCl}$) exactly.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Relative Melting Points
worked_examples.problem: Arrange in order of increasing melting point: $\text{NaCl}$, $\text{CH}_4$, $\text{SiO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$. Explain your reasoning.

step_by_step.explanation: $\text{NaCl}$: ionic; $\text{CH}_4$: covalent molecular; $\text{SiO}_2$: covalent network; $\text{H}_2\text{O}$: covalent molecular with H-bonding.
Covalent network > ionic lattice > H-bonding molecular > LDF molecular.

worked_examples.final_answer: Increasing melting point: $\text{CH}_4$ ($-182^\circ\text{C}$) < $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ ($0^\circ\text{C}$) < $\text{NaCl}$ ($801^\circ\text{C}$) < $\text{SiO}_2$ ($1710^\circ\text{C}$). Covalent network ($\text{SiO}_2$) requires breaking covalent bonds; ionic ($\text{NaCl}$) requires overcoming lattice energy; molecular compounds only require overcoming intermolecular forces.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Bonding Type and Melting Point Prediction

worked_examples.title: Explaining Conductivity Differences
worked_examples.problem: Explain why solid $\text{KBr}$ doesn't conduct electricity but $\text{KBr}(aq)$ does. Why doesn't sugar solution conduct?

step_by_step.explanation: $\text{KBr}$ is ionic; in solid state, $\text{K}^+$ and $\text{Br}^-$ are fixed in lattice positions and cannot move.
step_by_step.calculation: Solid ionic: ions fixed → no conductivity

step_by_step.explanation: In water, $\text{KBr}$ dissociates: $\text{KBr}(s) \rightarrow \text{K}^+(aq) + \text{Br}^-(aq)$. Ions are mobile and carry current.
Sugar ($\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{22}\text{O}_{11}$) is covalent molecular; dissolves as intact molecules, no ions produced.
step_by_step.calculation: Covalent molecular: no ions → no conductivity

worked_examples.final_answer: Solid $\text{KBr}$ doesn't conduct because ions are locked in the crystal lattice and cannot move. $\text{KBr}(aq)$ conducts because the compound dissociates into mobile $\text{K}^+$ and $\text{Br}^-$ ions that carry electrical current. Sugar solution doesn't conduct because sugar dissolves as neutral molecules without producing ions—no charge carriers exist.

worked_examples.title: Solubility Prediction
worked_examples.problem: Predict whether each substance will dissolve in water: (a) $\text{CaCl}_2$, (b) $\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}$ (octane), (c) $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$ (methanol). Explain.

step_by_step.explanation: Ionic compound with $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ and $\text{Cl}^-$. Chlorides are generally soluble (except Ag, Pb, Hg). Ion-dipole interactions with water favor dissolution.
Nonpolar covalent hydrocarbon; only LDF. Water-water H-bonds are much stronger than water-octane interactions. Doesn't dissolve.
Polar covalent with $-\text{OH}$ group; can form H-bonds with water. Miscible in all proportions.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) $\text{CaCl}_2$: soluble (ionic, favorable ion-dipole interactions); (b) Octane: insoluble (nonpolar, can't compete with water H-bonds); (c) Methanol: soluble (polar, forms H-bonds with water). The 'like dissolves like' principle correctly predicts all three cases.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Solubility Prediction Based on Bonding and Polarity

worked_examples.title: Mechanical Property Explanation
worked_examples.problem: Why can copper be drawn into wires but salt ($\text{NaCl}$) crystals shatter when struck? Explain using bonding concepts.

step_by_step.explanation: Copper has metallic bonding: $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ ions in sea of delocalized electrons. Bonds are non-directional.
step_by_step.calculation: Metallic: electron sea allows layer sliding

step_by_step.explanation: When stress is applied, ion layers slide; electron sea readjusts instantly, maintaining cohesion. No fracture occurs.
$\text{NaCl}$ has ionic bonding: alternating $\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{Cl}^-$ in rigid lattice. Bonds are directional (electrostatic).
When struck, layers shift, bringing like charges adjacent ($\text{Na}^+$-$\text{Na}^+$ or $\text{Cl}^-$-$\text{Cl}^-$). Repulsion causes cleavage.

worked_examples.final_answer: Copper is ductile because metallic bonding is non-directional: the electron sea maintains cohesion even as ion layers slide past each other. $\text{NaCl}$ is brittle because ionic bonding is directional: when layers shift, like-charged ions align and repel, causing the crystal to fracture along cleavage planes.

summary: Ionic and covalent compounds exhibit dramatically different properties due to their fundamental structural differences. Ionic compounds form crystal lattices with strong electrostatic forces, giving high melting points ($400-3000^\circ\text{C}$), conductivity when molten/dissolved (mobile ions), water solubility (ion-dipole interactions), and brittleness (like-charge repulsion on deformation). Covalent molecular compounds form discrete molecules with weak intermolecular forces, giving low melting points ($< 300^\circ\text{C}$), no conductivity (no charge carriers), variable solubility (polar dissolves in polar), and softness. Covalent network solids (diamond, quartz) are exceptions with very high melting points and hardness due to extended covalent bonding. The 'like dissolves like' principle predicts solubility: ionic/polar solutes dissolve in polar solvents; nonpolar solutes dissolve in nonpolar solvents. These property differences enable practical identification and application of compounds in chemistry, materials science, and biology.
